message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Poliovirus vaccination induces an adaptive humoral immune response;
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    experiments show polio-immune sera contain antibodies against the poliovirus RNA transcriptase that cross-react with SARS-CoV-2. While structural similarities between poliovirus and SARS-CoV-2 could have major implications for the COVID-19 response worldwide, polio-induced immune responses against SARS-CoV-2 have not been confirmed in prospective clinical trials.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate whether immune sera from adults who recently received inactivated poliovirus vaccination (IPV) can block SARS-CoV-2’s ability to synthesize RNA.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>IPV intramuscular injection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Measurements</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Pre-inoculation and 4-weeks post-inoculation sera were tested for anti-3D
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>pol</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    (RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, RdRp) antibodies using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA). To assess IPV’s ability to induce antibodies that inhibit SARS-CoV-2 RNA synthesis, immune-based detection assays tested RdRp enzymatic activity in polio-immune sera.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    298 of the 300 enrolled participants completed both on-site visits. Comparing pre-inoculation to 4-week samples, 85.2% of participants demonstrated an increase in anti-3D
                    <jats:sup>
                      <jats:italic>pol</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sup>
                    antibodies against RdRp proteins. Among tested post-inoculation samples, 94.4% demonstrated inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 RNA synthesis. Few inoculation-related side effects were reported (2.0%), all were minor.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Limitations</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Participants were not systematically tested for COVID-19, though known exposures were reported and positive results (1.7%) were documented.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>IPV can induce antibodies that inhibit SARS-CoV-2 RNA synthesis, minimizing the risk of viral replication in infected individuals. This finding has practical implications for resource-deficient areas that may have limited access to newly developed COVID-19 vaccines and/or areas with low COVID-19 vaccination rates due to hesitancy.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding Source</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Private donors.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="http://ClinicalTrials.gov">ClinicalTrials.gov</jats:ext-link>
                    :
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04639375">NCT04639375</jats:ext-link>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine Induces Antibodies that Inhibit RNA Synthesis of SARS-CoV-2: An open-label, pre-post vaccine clinical trial

reference.unstructured: Johns Hopkins University. COVID-19 Dash-board by the Center for Systems Science and Engineering (CSSE). Johns Hopkins Coronavirus Resource Center, Johns Hopkins University. Updated October 3, 2021. Accessed October 3, 2021. https://coronavirus.jhu.edu/map.html.

Holder J. Tracking Coronavirus Vaccinations Around the World. New York Times. Updated October 3, 2021. Accessed October 3, 2021. https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2021/world/covid-vaccinations-tracker.html

World Health Organization. Persistence of protective antibodies following immunization with OPV and IPV. World Health Organization. Updated 2015. Accessed October 3, 2021. https://www.who.int/immunization/polio_grad_duration_protection.pdf

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Polio Vaccination Recommendations for Specific Groups. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Updated May 4, 2018. Accessed October 3, 2021. https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/vpd/polio/hcp/recommendations.html
